main_summary.summary: Judy Richardson, interviewed December 9, 2015 by Emilye Crosby in Silver Spring, Maryland, recounts her path from Tarrytown, NY, to SNCC's Atlanta National Office, then to film work with Blackside on Eyes on the Prize. Born March 10, 1944, to a UAW organizer father at the Tarrytown Fisher Body Chevrolet plant and a jazz-pianist, numbers-booking mother, Richardson entered Swarthmore College in 1962 as one of eight Black freshmen, joined the SDS chapter SPAC, and through it traveled to Cambridge, Maryland to picket the Choptank Inn and the segregated skating rink alongside Gloria Richardson Dandridge and the Cambridge Non-Violent Action Committee. After Reggie Robinson recruited her, James Forman conscripted her in fall 1963 as his secretary in SNCC's Atlanta office, where she took executive-committee minutes, ran the mimeograph in a roach-filled work room, transcribed Prathia Hall speeches, and coordinated WATS-line emergency calls. She describes Jack Minnis's Mississippi Chronology of Violence and Intimidation as the template she later used at the United Church of Christ's Commission for Racial Justice and on Eyes on the Prize. She also recounts the women's sit-in in Forman's office demanding rotation off secretarial duties.
key_themes: Women's labor and authority within SNCC
Black church and freedom-song movement culture
main_summary.historical_significance:
Richardson's interview is a primary source on the day-to-day administrative infrastructure of SNCC's Atlanta National Office at a level of specificity rarely captured: the layout of the 8 1/2 Raymond Street office, the WATS-line emergency-call protocol (person-to-person operator workarounds, friends-of-SNCC follow-up calls to Mississippi sheriffs), the mimeograph assembly line, the executive-committee minutes process, and the bookkeeping discipline Ruby Doris Smith Robinson imposed on field-staff stipends. She is one of few SNCC veterans to also have spent decades on the documentary side (Blackside's Eyes on the Prize series I and II, Malcolm X: Make It Plain), so her account connects movement memory to its later media construction.

The interview documents the women's sit-in in James Forman's Atlanta office that Richardson, Ruby Doris Smith Robinson, Mary King, Bobbi Yancy, and Mildred Forman staged with placards reading "No freedom, no minutes", a moment Forman later misattributed to his own suggestion, which Richardson corrects on the record. It also preserves first-person testimony on Gloria Richardson Dandridge's stance that Cambridge organizers were never philosophically nonviolent, and on the cold-war diplomatic pressure point of Oginga Odinga's Peachtree Manor Hotel stay that the recording cuts off mid-narration.

chapters.title: Closing the Curtains on the Union Man
chapters.summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby in Silver Spring, Maryland, on December 9, 2015, Judy Richardson opens with her 1944 birth in Tarrytown, New York, her seamstress and jazz-pianist mother Mellie Louise Richardson, and her father William King Richardson, who helped organize the United Auto Workers local at the Fisher Body Chevrolet plant, the family drawing the curtains around the national organizer and finding grocery bags left on the porch by neighbors too afraid to support him openly, until he died of a heart attack on the assembly line around 1951 when she was seven.

chapters.title: A Town Steeped in Sleepy Hollow
chapters.summary: Richardson sketches Tarrytown's roughly ten percent Black population, the working-class Italian, Polish, and Irish families under the hill near the plant, the Washington Irving and Sleepy Hollow schools, and her later return from SNCC to protest segregated housing alongside a Black doctor and architect couple who became the first to move onto Broadway.

chapters.title: Billy's House and the Blackball
chapters.summary: She recounts the picket line her group threw up at the all-white volunteer fire department, explaining the blackball that kept Black residents out, and a fireman who, when the family house caught fire on Thanksgiving, stopped another from taking a hatchet to the door because it was Billy's house.

chapters.title: Stokely in Patriots Park
chapters.summary: Back in Tarrytown around 1964 raising money for SNCC, Richardson brought Stokely Carmichael and the SNCC Freedom Singers, including Bernice Johnson Reagon, Chuck Neblett, Rutha Mae Harris, and Cordell Reagon, to Patriots Park, drawing a red-baiting letter from a Mrs. Kenney charging that Communists were singing in the park.

chapters.title: My Little Mommy's Blue Chords
chapters.summary: Richardson reads her mother's worried letters from the 1964 Mississippi period, written in beautiful hand despite an eighth-grade education, recalls returning to find her mother playing We Shall Overcome with gorgeous blue jazz chords she had heard rather than read, and describes her mother's earlier years playing piano with the Teddy Whiteley Orchestra in Westchester County until her father stopped her, and her lifelong love of Oscar Peterson and Art Tatum.

chapters.title: Booking the Numbers
chapters.topic: The Numbers and a Fur Coat
chapters.summary: She explains how her mother booked numbers, the lottery based on the day's horse races, for the ethnic neighborhood with Trippi as the runner, how her father was once arrested with number slips on him, and how her mother, after a win, drove to the Bronx to buy fur coats with her sister Helen.

chapters.title: She Paid Cash for the Valiant
chapters.summary: After her father died, Richardson recounts the UAW pension that gave her mother a modicum of security, one of the only two times she saw her mother address race, when a white salesman implied she could not afford a Valiant and she returned to pay cash outright, and how her mother kept scoring at the top of the county exam yet was hired only after the druggist Mr. Slaven told her to switch her registration to Republican, becoming a secretary in White Plains.

chapters.title: I Couldn't Lift My Head
chapters.topic: A Mother and Birth of a Nation
chapters.summary: Richardson recounts the second time her mother spoke of race, describing seeing Birth of a Nation around 1915 under President Wilson and being so shamed by its portrayal of Black people that she could only look down at the pavement leaving the theater, a film that valorized the Klan as protectors of white womanhood after Reconstruction.

chapters.title: The Same Illustration in My Textbook
chapters.topic: A Caricatured Reconstruction and Little Rock
chapters.summary: She describes the lone illustration of Black Reconstruction in her AP history textbook at Sleepy Hollow High School, drawn from Birth of a Nation, the white classmates glancing back at her, learning later from historian John Dittmer during eyes on the prize that it was a nationally prescribed text, and her own high-school letter to the Tarrytown Daily News questioning why the Little Rock students were trying to enter a white school.

chapters.title: Four Black Men, Four Black Women
chapters.summary: Richardson describes entering Swarthmore in 1962 as one of eight Black freshmen, four men and four women, after Bennington offered admission without money while Swarthmore offered full freight for four years, all of them housed with Quaker children on the assumption the Quakers would be more accepting.

chapters.title: The Boy Who Knew Lenin and Trotsky
chapters.summary: She contrasts her own background, one plane ride to Buffalo with the all-state youth orchestra, with classmates who had wintered in Paris and come from schools like Sidwell Friends, and recalls respecting a Poli Sci classmate who knew Lenin and Trotsky yet would later say the Black students should stick to their education.

chapters.title: Six Weeks in Parchman
chapters.summary: Richardson recounts her first SDS meeting through the Swarthmore Political Action Committee, SPAC, co-chaired by Mimi Feingold and Carl Wittman, learning only decades later that Feingold had done six weeks in Parchman on the Freedom Rides as a CORE staffer before standing up at that meeting, later going into deadly Plaquemines Parish, Louisiana.

chapters.title: H2O Backwards on My Thigh
chapters.summary: She lays out SPAC's three programs, organizing the all-Black cafeteria staff, tutorials in Chester, Pennsylvania, and supporting Gloria Richardson Dandridge in Cambridge, and tells how, as a work-study waitress, she was branded with H2O backwards on her thigh by the uncovered hot water pipe the staff had complained of, an injury Mimi Feingold turned at once toward opening conditions for the strong all-Black women's weight staff.

chapters.title: I Just Get on the Bus
chapters.summary: Richardson describes sampling everything as a freshman away from her mother, signing up for the weekend bus to Cambridge, Maryland that left Saturday and returned Sunday night for Monday classes, joining students from Morgan State and other colleges to picket the Choptank Inn at the Old Barn Grill and the skating rink.

chapters.title: Little White Gloves at the National Office
chapters.topic: Gloria Richardson and the Roller Rink
chapters.summary: She recounts the dank Old Barn Grill she wondered why she was trying to integrate, and how Gloria Richardson Dandridge and a relative, wearing little white gloves and heels, walked into SNCC's overalls-and-jeans national office seeking help to organize the roller-rink campaign their young people had started, where Reggie Robinson became the first SNCC field secretary Richardson met.

chapters.title: I Tripped One of Them
chapters.summary: Richardson explains that Gloria Richardson was never philosophically or even tactically nonviolent off the picket line, recounting how Gloria tripped a white man she had grown up with who called her names, and how Race Street divided white and Black Cambridge, where the community kept and used guns to defend itself.

chapters.title: The Camaraderie Was So Amazing
chapters.summary: She describes the energy of being part of a group of young Black and white people her age doing something about what she had sublimated all her life in Tarrytown, a town with no Black middle class until Cab Calloway's daughter Chris briefly enrolled, contrasted with the Black-owned businesses she saw in Cambridge.

chapters.title: You Lift Up to Another Place
chapters.topic: Mass Meetings and a Bible Camp
chapters.summary: Richardson recalls the freedom songs at Black-church mass meetings lifting her up though she was not religious, her mother who read the Bible but hated organized religion, and the white Baptist Bible camp she quit after overhearing members say they had to be careful not to let too many Black children come in and get overrun.

chapters.part: A Year Off for the Movement
chapters.title: Go By Ruby Doris
chapters.topic: Penny Patch and Taking a Year Off
chapters.summary: Richardson tells how Penny Patch, just back from the Albany, Georgia SNCC project, came to her dorm room and urged her to take her sophomore fall off to work full-time with SNCC in Cambridge, warning that as a Northerner she would have to apply and get past Ruby Doris Smith Robinson, the administrator in the Atlanta national office.

chapters.part: A Year Off for the Movement
chapters.title: Kita Screaming on the Hall Phone
chapters.summary: She recounts calling her mother from the dorm hall phone to say she would keep her full scholarship while working with SNCC, her mother amazingly calm, then being called back to find her older sister Carita, who worked in the A&R division at Columbia Records, screaming that she could not sacrifice her education, until Richardson convinced her too.

chapters.part: A Year Off for the Movement
chapters.title: My Sister Could Talk to Anybody
chapters.summary: Richardson remembers her mother and sister Carita driving her to Swarthmore the first time, her mother sensing the prestige of the rolling hills though she had never seen Bennington, being allowed to drive through the tunnel and changing lanes, and her shy self eased by a sister who could talk to anyone and paved the way in the dorm before her mother returned to an empty house.

chapters.part: A Year Off for the Movement
chapters.title: A Princess in a Crown of Roses
chapters.topic: The May Queen and a Divided Campus
chapters.summary: She describes SPAC running a block-voting slate for the May Queen ceremony that usually went to a fraternity sweetheart, electing Mimi Feingold as May Queen and Richardson as a princess in a crown of roses, her proud mother coming down unaware that fraternity boys were boycotting and that the Cambridge demonstrations had split the campus, with the student paper quoting normally liberal students that the Negro students should concentrate on their education.

chapters.part: A Year Off for the Movement
chapters.title: Same Campus, Different Organizations
chapters.summary: She reflects on how she came into the movement through Cambridge while Mimi Feingold was already CORE from the Freedom Rides and Brooklyn CORE, the two ending up in different organizations, then finished her summer job at a foster-kids halfway house and headed into Cambridge in October to continue the work.

chapters.title: Maid of Honor for a Stranger
chapters.summary: Richardson recounts Reggie Robinson inviting her on the train to Cincinnati for the wedding of field secretary Bill Hansen, who worked in Arkansas, and Ruthie, the interracial couple pouring her into a little black dress and heels to stand as maid of honor for people she had never met, with a stop planned at the national office on the way back.

chapters.title: Hey, Captain
chapters.summary: She describes expecting an Urban League office with rugs and suits and instead finding a man in overalls sweeping the national office stairs on Raymond Street, greeting Reggie like a long-lost brother, who turned out to be executive secretary James Forman, who swept so everyone would see no job was too lowly for anyone in SNCC.

chapters.title: Ninety Words a Minute
chapters.summary: Richardson tells how Forman, learning she could take shorthand and type ninety words a minute, skills forced on her when Washington Irving Junior High shifted its top Black student into the secretarial track, made her his secretary and moved her into the Freedom House, and recalls her first staff meeting in November 1963 just as Kennedy was shot, with Dinky Romilly the one she remembers crying as the staff worked out a strategic response.

chapters.title: Up the Stairs and to the Left
chapters.summary: Richardson walks through the Atlanta office, the reception area where staff took turns and Jimmy Bolton worked, Julian Bond as communications director with Mary King his assistant, Bobbi Yancy heading the campus travelers to the Black colleges, and a colleague doing related work with the Black campuses.

chapters.title: Research From a Stone
chapters.topic: Jack Minnis and the WATS Line
chapters.summary: She introduces Jack Minnis, the research-department head who could pull research from a stone, and the Mississippi chronology of violence and intimidation since 1961 he compiled from WATS-line reports, explaining the person-to-person collect-call trick by which the office learned who was in trouble before calling back free, until white Mississippi operators caught on and cut the calls off.

chapters.title: An Occupying Army
chapters.summary: Richardson traces how Jack Minnis's chronology lesson shaped her later career, building a pattern of police-brutality cases for a Los Angeles film company and as information director of the United Church of Christ Commission for Racial Justice, citing Eleanor Bumpurs and Michael Stewart, and using the pattern in eyes on the prize on the Fred Hampton and Mark Clark assassinations in the Nation of Law episode.

chapters.title: I Was Trained by the Movement
chapters.summary: She recounts the Detroit uprising over a closed blind pig, persuading writer Steve Fayer and producer Orlando Bagwell to keep the narration that police are an occupying army in Malcolm X: Make It Plain, and a New York Newsday reporter, surprised by the chronology in her press packet, asking if she was a reporter, to which she answered that she had been trained by the movement.

chapters.title: The Movement Bypassed St. Louis
chapters.summary: Richardson tells of screening her Orangeburg massacre film at Washington University after Ferguson and meeting young people from the Organization for Black Struggle, who said the movement had bypassed St. Louis and described the fur-coated Black glitterati who appear each King Day, and of urging on them the value of statistics, how many police are charged or go to trial, as evidence that cannot be denied.

chapters.title: Our Own Printing Press
chapters.topic: The Student Voice and Friends of SNCC
chapters.summary: Richardson describes the printing press in the national-office basement run by Wilson Brown so SNCC never depended on others, the Student Voice mailed to the offices, and the Friends of SNCC network coordinated by Dinky Romilly and then Betty Garman, like Mrs. Shaw in Chicago, who would phone a Mississippi sheriff so white supremacists knew outsiders were watching, the field staff calling in once a day to the national office and twice a day into Jackson by the summer of 1964.

chapters.title: Get Me Your Field Reports
chapters.topic: Ruby Doris and the Monthly Reports
chapters.summary: Richardson recounts Ruby Doris Smith Robinson's insistence on monthly field reports as the condition for the ten-dollar weekly pay that came to $9.64 after taxes, and Ivanhoe Donaldson's account that while people respected Forman's administrative sense, they knew Ruby truly cared, sending him a letter every day he sat in a Clarksdale jail.

chapters.title: She Did Not Suffer Fools
chapters.topic: Ruby's Presence and a Lean Week
chapters.summary: She describes Ruby Doris Smith Robinson's history, Parchman and thirty days no bail in Rock Hill, South Carolina, and her presence akin to Ella Baker, a baby shower for the pregnant Ruby where the SNCC women fell into long silences because they did not do small talk, and a week in Cordele, Georgia when her own $9.64 did not come and she asked an unfriendly NAACP man for credit on a hamburger while playing Dionne Warwick's Walk On By.

chapters.title: A Bird's-Eye View
chapters.summary: Richardson explains how Forman gave her as much responsibility as he thought she could take, a bird's-eye view of the whole organization, taking executive-meeting minutes so she knew who was in trouble, sitting at a tiny desk by his office near Dinky Romilly and the bookkeeper, and writing donor letters she signed with Forman's name.

chapters.title: Prathia Hall Lifted You Up
chapters.summary: She describes transcribing a Prathia Hall speech, probably from Selma, off a reel-to-reel onto an IBM Selectric with tears streaming, recalling that even Dr. King disliked following Prathia Hall, who came out of a Pentecostal Black church in Philadelphia, and how Hall got on the case of SCLC field staff who told local people after Bloody Sunday they could not demonstrate unless they were philosophically nonviolent, calling it blackmail.

chapters.title: Singing on the Assembly Line
chapters.summary: She describes the workroom assembly line stapling and collating press releases off the mimeograph, everyone singing freedom songs because the work went quicker, and the executive committee meetings of field staff held in Frasier's basement or other spaces when the office was too small, sometimes fed by the host when money was short.

chapters.title: Thirty-Three Pages of Minutes
chapters.topic: In Awe of the SNCC People
chapters.summary: Richardson recalls her first executive-committee minutes running thirty-three pages because she was in awe of the SNCC people, the Howard NAG members who cited Nietzsche and the Southern young people who knew Black history she did not, typing onto long green stencils corrected with finicky green fluid, then steeling herself to run them off alone late at night in a workroom full of flying water bugs, her diary noting the birds singing as she finished and mailed them to the field.

chapters.title: I Want to Go to the Field
chapters.topic: Who Staffed the Office and Why
chapters.summary: Richardson tells how she kept asking Forman to send her to the field in Mississippi while he resisted losing a good secretary, and explains that white staff, especially white women whose presence could get Black men killed, were placed in the national office, with Charles Sherrod's integrated Southwest Georgia staff the deliberate exception.

chapters.title: Birmingham the Day the Girls Died
chapters.summary: She describes the national office's photo department with Danny Lyon, and the arrival of Tamio Wakayama, who reached Birmingham the day the four girls were killed in the church bombing, was steered to safety by a Black resident, found Forman, Danny Lyon, and Julian Bond, and drove them to Atlanta before joining the photo department.

chapters.title: No Minutes Until Freedom Comes
chapters.summary: Richardson recounts the staged protest while Forman was away at an anti-apartheid meeting in London, when she, Ruby Doris Smith Robinson, Mildred Forman, Mary King, and Bobbi Yancy sat with little placards reading no minutes until freedom comes to the Atlanta office, photographed by Danny Lyon when Forman returned.

chapters.title: No, I Have the Facts
chapters.summary: She describes Forman's second edition of his memoir claiming he had suggested the women do the sit-in, her exclamation-pointed disagreement, confronting him alone at a book party, and at the Duke SNCC fortieth anniversary panel, where he said Judy had another interpretation and she replied that she had the facts but he was the one telling the story.

chapters.title: Limitless Within the Agenda
chapters.summary: Richardson reflects on feeling limitless because the organization let her act, proposing a residential freedom school around 1965 to join the energy of Southern young people with Northern Friends-of-SNCC youth in Chicago and Cordele, which Ivanhoe Donaldson helped fund and Stokely Carmichael and Charlie Cobb offered to support, no one telling her she had never done it before.

chapters.part: The Toddle House and Oginga Odinga
chapters.title: A Diplomat at the Peachtree Manor
chapters.topic: The Toddle House Sit-In and the Cold War
chapters.summary: Richardson closes on the Atlanta sit-ins where she was arrested several times, and the day at the Toddle House when Forman learned the Kenyan official Oginga Odinga, serving under Jomo Kenyatta, was staying at the recently integrated Peachtree Manor Hotel, set against the Cold War embarrassment of African diplomats driving Route 40 between New York and Washington unable to eat once past Delaware, a segregation the United States could not afford as it courted newly independent African nations against Russia.

loc_video.caption: Judy Richardson oral history interview conducted by Emilye Crosby in Silver Spring, Maryland, 2015 December 09
